--- title: RIP pandas, welcome Polars frontpic: img/polars_vs_pandas.png frontpicwidth: 40% frontpicmargintop: 30px frontpicmarginbottom: 20px author: Marie-Hélène Burle date: 2026-04-7 date-format: long execute: freeze: auto cache: true error: true echo: true format: revealjs: theme: [default, ../revealjspdpl.scss] logo: /img/favicon_sfudrac.png highlight-style: vim-dark code-line-numbers: false template-partials: - ../title-slide.html pointer: color: "#b5111b" pointerSize: 32 link-external-newwindow: true footer:  Back to webinar page auto-stretch: false revealjs-plugins: - pointer --- ## Goal of this webinar {.center} There is a new and much better library for Python DataFrames There are no downsides to using it, beside the effort to changing habits. For new users who don't have habits yet, there are no downsides Yet, all Python intro courses still teach the old tool [My goal is to help shift the culture towards a wide adoption of Polars instead of pandas]{.emph} ## A brief history of DataFrames {.center} ```{dot} //| echo: false //| fig-height: 450px strict graph { bgcolor="transparent" graph [fontname="Inconsolata, sans-serif"] node [fontname="Inconsolata, sans-serif", fontsize=15] y1 [label=1990, shape=plaintext, group=g1, group=g1] y2 [label=2000, shape=plaintext, group=g1, group=g1] y3 [label=2008, shape=plaintext, group=g1] y4 [label=2020, shape=plaintext, group=g1] l1 [label="S language introduces concept of DataFrames", href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S_(programming_language)", shape=plaintext, group=g2] l2 [label="R supercedes S", href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/R_(programming_language)", shape=plaintext, group=g2] l3 [label="DataFrames ported to Python with pandas", href="https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas", shape=plaintext, group=g2, fontcolor="#e84c44"] l4 [label="Better DataFrames library for Python: Polars", href="https://github.com/pola-rs/polars", shape=plaintext, group=g2, fontcolor="#69c9e6"] {rank=same; y1 l1} y1 -- y2 -- y3 -- y4 l1 -- l2 -- l3 -- l4 [style=invis] } ``` ## Overview {.center} | | ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="55%"} || ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="50%"} | |--|--|--|--| | Available for | Python || Rust, Python, R, NodeJS | | Written in | Cython || Rust | | Multithreading | Some operations || Yes (GIL released) | | Index | Rows are indexed || Integer positions are used | | Evaluation | Eager || Eager and lazy | | Query optimizer | No || Yes | | Out-of-core | No || Yes | | [SIMD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_instruction,_multiple_data) vectorization | Yes || Yes | | Data in memory | [NumPy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy) arrays || [Apache Arrow](https://github.com/apache/arrow) arrays | | Memory efficiency | Poor || Excellent | | Missing data handling | Inconsistent || Consistent; type stability | ## Method {.center} The performance of tools can only be evaluated on optimized code: poorly written pandas code is a lot slower than efficient pandas code I use the best pandas code from [Alex Razoumov pandas course](https://wgpages.netlify.app/python2/python-13-pandas/) in which he benchmarks various syntaxes # Example 1: a classic ## FizzBuzz {.center} ## {.center} Load libraries: ::: {#1824b679 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import numpy as np import polars as pl ``` ::: Set series length: ::: {#8d627927 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} length = 10_000 ``` ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#db9cb388 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd = pd.DataFrame() df_pd["Count"] = np.arange(1, length+1) df_pd["FizzBuzz"] = df_pd["Count"].astype(str) df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 3 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Fizz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 5 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Buzz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 15 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "FizzBuzz" ``` ::: ## {.center} ::: {#a2bb60f4 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
Count FizzBuzz
0 1 1
1 2 2
2 3 Fizz
3 4 4
4 5 Buzz
... ... ...
9995 9996 Fizz
9996 9997 9997
9997 9998 9998
9998 9999 Fizz
9999 10000 Buzz

10000 rows × 2 columns

``` ::: ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#e3260acd .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl = pl.DataFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, length+1)}) df_pl.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl = df_pl.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) ``` ::: ## {.center} ::: {#acb9cb2d .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}
shape: (10_000, 2)
CountFizzBuzz
i64str
1"1"
2"2"
3"Fizz"
4"4"
5"Buzz"
……
9996"Fizz"
9997"9997"
9998"9998"
9999"Fizz"
10000"Buzz"
``` ::: ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#e772b3b2 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy = pl.LazyFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, length+1)}) df_pl_lazy.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl_lazy = df_pl_lazy.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) df_pl_lazy_collected = df_pl_lazy.collect() ``` ::: ## {.center} ::: {#ca5f0d67 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy_collected ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ```{=html}
shape: (10_000, 2)
CountFizzBuzz
i64str
1"1"
2"2"
3"Fizz"
4"4"
5"Buzz"
……
9996"Fizz"
9997"9997"
9998"9998"
9999"Fizz"
10000"Buzz"
``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#552de24d .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pd = pd.DataFrame() df_pd["Count"] = np.arange(1, length+1) df_pd["FizzBuzz"] = df_pd["Count"].astype(str) df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 3 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Fizz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 5 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Buzz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 15 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "FizzBuzz" ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 3.45 ms ± 171 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#cea96202 .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pl = pl.DataFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, length+1)}) df_pl.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 657 μs ± 15.5 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#091093cf .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pl_lazy = pl.LazyFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, length+1)}) df_pl_lazy.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl_lazy = df_pl_lazy.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) df_pl = df_pl_lazy.collect() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 464 μs ± 7.35 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each) ``` ::: ::: ## Speedup {.center} Speedup with Polars DataFrame around 6 \ With Polars LazyFrame around 8 These speedups highly depend on the types and sizes of the problems How much faster Polars is does vary, what doesn't vary is that it is always much faster ## Memory usage {.center} ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="32%"} **pandas** ::: {#e844edf5 .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd.memory_usage() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ``` Index 132 Count 80000 FizzBuzz 80000 dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: 132 + 80,000 * 2 = \ 160,132 bytes \ **≈ 160 KB** ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="32%"} **Polars** ::: {#6aa9c895 .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl.estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=13} ``` 119409 ``` ::: ::: 119,409 bytes \ **≈ 119 KB** 160,132 / 119,409 ≈ 1.3 \ Footprint 1.3 times smaller ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="32%"} **Polars lazy**
Same as Polars eager: \ if you collect the entire DataFrame as we did here, the lazy API does not reduce the memory footprint ::: :::: # Example 2: medium dataset ## Data characteristics {.center} Shape: 216,930 rows, 7 columns ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#894549c8 .cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd = pd.read_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pd_subset = df_pd.loc[df_pd["Category"] == "HISTORY"] df_pd_subset.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=14} ``` (349, 7) ``` ::: ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#19dba8d0 .cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl = pl.read_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pl_subset = df_pl.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY") df_pl_subset.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=15} ``` (349, 7) ``` ::: ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#522a5317 .cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pl_lazy_subset_collected = df_pl_lazy.filter( pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY" ).collect() df_pl_lazy_subset_collected.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=16} ``` (349, 7) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#4e78ca93 .cell execution_count=17} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pd = pd.read_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pd.loc[df_pd["Category"] == "HISTORY"].shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 1.18 s ± 20.2 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#d71bc9b1 .cell execution_count=18} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pl = pl.read_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pl.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 802 ms ± 33 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#901360db .cell execution_count=19} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pl_lazy.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").collect().shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 66.3 ms ± 3.56 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each) ``` ::: ::: ## Speedup {.center} Polars here brings a speedup around 1.5 Polars lazy ramps that up to a speedup of 17 ## Memory usage {.center} ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="30%"} ::: {#ebb33239 .cell execution_count=20} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd.memory_usage() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=20} ``` Index 132 Show Number 1735440 Air Date 1735440 Round 1735440 Category 1735440 Value 1735440 Question 1735440 Answer 1735440 dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: 132 + 1,735,440 * 7 = \ 12,148,212 bytes \ **≈ 12 MB** ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="33%"} ::: {#ec62d456 .cell execution_count=21} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl.estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=21} ``` 31606250 ``` ::: ::: 31,606,250 bytes \ **≈ 31 MB** 31,606,250 / 12,148,212 ≈ 2.6 \ Footprint 2.6 times *bigger* ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="33%"} ::: {#70d8ce10 .cell execution_count=22} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy_subset_collected.estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=22} ``` 48774 ``` ::: ::: 48,774 bytes \ **≈ 49 KB** 12,148,212 / 48,774 ≈ 249 \ Footprint **249 times smaller** If you only collect a subset of the data, the memory footprint drops dramatically ::: :::: # Example 3:
data too big to fit in memory ## Data characteristics {.center}
